channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: PlayStation Pricing Is On Trial Again, As Sony Fights For 30-Percent Fees
item.content:encoded:
Back in 2022 we first reported on the news that a consumer rights group in the UK was attempting to launch the British equivalent of a class action lawsuit (it’s the same but with more “u”s) against Sony on the basis that the company charges a 30-percent commission fee on all digital purchases with no option for competition. After failing to get the multi-billion dollar “PlayStation You Owe Us” lawsuit thrown out in 2023, Sony’s time in court comes to an end this week. And if this all sounds very familiar, yes, it was only this weekend that Sony lost a similar case in California.

The argument in both cases is that by being the sole store and distributor for digital purchases on the PlayStation, Sony is able to enforce its 30-percent cut of every sale without fear of being undercut by others, although in the U.S. version this was more specifically focused on vouchers sold by other outlets. Were other stores able to sell digital PS4 and PS5 games, there would be market competition, the UK case claims, and without it customers are therefore being overcharged. And, yeah, that seems pretty true. The Northern District of California certainly suggested there’s merit to the claims, ruling that Sony owed those who’d bought specific games on PSN a total of $7.8 billion, although it didn’t rule that any practices were illegal. Come the end of this week, Sony will have to just sit and wait to find out if it’s going to be hemorrhaging cash on both sides of the pond.

As covered by The Game Business, the UK’s $2.6 billion lawsuit covers those who bought digital PSN games between August 2016 and February 2026, based on the same monopolistic claims and its 30-percent charge. Sony has countered by arguing that there’s plenty of competition actually (although this is somewhat spurious as the logic only applies to cross-platform releases and not PlayStation exclusives) and that the 30-percent tithe keeps hardware costs down (a tougher pill to swallow after PlayStation prices saw a massive hike just over a month ago). The Japanese company attempted to have the case thrown out in late 2023, but its appeal was unsuccessful.

This of course also brings to mind the case against Apple’s 30-percent cut in another British case, where the ruling went against the big firm and it ended up being required to pay out £1.5 billion ($2 billion) in damages to UK customers. But as The Game Business observes, in Apple’s case the garden walls are a lot higher than Sony’s, where the company could more successfully argue that cross-platform competition keeps prices “honest.” (Although having just paid $95 for Saros in the UK, I’m not exactly convinced.) And it’s hard not to think about Epic’s recent cases against Apple and Google, which also focused on the lack of competitive digital store options and 30-percent cuts.

A possible outcome of the UK case is Sony being required to lower its 30-percent fee, which could lead to lower prices (publishers and indies would therefore receive a greater proportion of the sale price, and thus could charge less to begin with), but given Sony’s willingness to just keep raising the price of a PlayStation it likely wouldn’t end anywhere particularly wonderful for consumers. Either way, it will likely take at least three months for the glacial courts to produce a ruling, and could be as long as a year-and-a-half.



https://kotaku.com/sony-playstation-prices-you-owe-us-lawsuit-ruling-fees-2000693593
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: It’s Been 365 Days Since We Last Saw GTA 6
item.content:encoded:
It’s kind of crazy to think that Grand Theft Auto 6, the game so big it feels like business analysts are putting its success at the center of conversations on the longevity of the video game industry, was once supposed to be out in just a few weeks. Before Rockstar delayed the game to November 19, Grand Theft Auto 6 was supposed to be launching on May 26. The company then announced it wanted another six months to work on the game, and while fans were not happy about it, the company seems pretty confident it won’t slip once more. What’s also wild is that as of today, May 6, we haven’t seen hide nor hair of the game in a full year.

Grand Theft Auto 6 has only had two trailers, and the last one we got premiered on May 6, 2025. With just two minutes and 46 seconds, Rockstar gave fans something to chew on and has been mostly radio silent on the game since. Unsurprisingly, fans who are eagerly anticipating the game have started to gnaw at the bars of their enclosures, desperate for a new look at the life and times of lovers Jason and Lucia. They’re gathering evidence that a third trailer could be coming any day now. Though both existing trailers do a good job of setting the tone for the romantic crime drama, we still haven’t seen Grand Theft Auto 6 actually being played, even with the game six months away from launch. 

At least this situation gave me an excuse to rewatch the scene of Jason fixing the “leaks” in nothing but cargo shorts and a backwards cap. Here’s hoping we see more such views between now and GTA 6’s November 19 launch.



https://kotaku.com/grand-theft-auto-6-gta6-new-trailer-rockstar-jason-lucia-2000693607
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Diablo 4′s Secret Cow Level Has Finally Been Found, So Now Obviously Players Are Trying To Summon The Cow God
item.content:encoded:
Diablo 4 has been out for nearly three years, with two entire expansions under its belt. I admit I haven’t been paying super-close attention to it recently, and I just sort of assumed that it, like all the other Diablos, had a Secret Cow Level that players had discovered within a few weeks of launch and that everyone had promptly laughed about and moved on. I was surprised to discover, then, that said Secret Cow Level was actually just found yesterday.

Diablo games have a long, storied history of cow levels. The idea of a cow level originated as a then-false rumor about the first Diablo, that a secret level could be reached by clicking on the game’s cows enough and entering a portal. The devs found the rumor amusing enough to start making cow-related in-game gags about it, and in Diablo 2 an actual secret level called Moo Moo Farm, complete with a Cow King boss, was added. Diablo 3 had multiple similar riffs on the theme, and Blizzard has slipped cow level and cow king references into its other games, too, like World of Warcraft.

Ever since Diablo 4 launched, players have been looking for the cow level that surely must be in the game. And their theories were far from mere speculation. Players managed to compile all sorts of weird clues that a cow level was waiting for them somewhere: there’s a cow-shaped map in one spot, someone found a special item by killing exactly 666 cows, those sorts of things.

But the Cow Level quest never fully coalesced until now. That’s because the Cow Level is not only rather difficult and obtuse to find, but it also necessitated some information and quest items only obtainable in the most recent expansion, Lord of Hatred. LoatheBurger, a YouTuber who has helped lead the charge in finding the cow level, has compiled videos on exactly how to complete all the Cow Quests that lead to the Cow Level, three in all. I watched his explanations through and every aspect of this sounds like a massive, ridiculous conspiracy theory. I have no idea how anyone solved it. Initially, the resolution to these quests seems to be a dud, as combining all three quest rewards grants you an achievement called “There Is No Cow Level,” echoing Blizzard’s ongoing cheeky insistence that the game had no cow level, and the location on the other side of the quest is just a cow-themed island with more cryptic imagery, such as 12 cows standing in a circle and a bunch of human skeletons gazing at a cow statue.

But players knew better. It’d be no fun at all if that were the end. Not long after all these clues were assembled, LoatheBurger solved the riddle of Cow Island and made it to the Cow Level just yesterday:

But that might not be the end of the mystery.

The Cow Level itself does, as is tradition, include a boss called The Cow King who looks exactly like you think he does. Defeating him drops a mythic helm entitled the Cow King’s Crown, which includes a bunch of absurd affixes dependent on what day of the week it is, some of which make no sense like “Wednesday: Chance to reduce enemy Cooldown (No)” and “Saturday: +1.3% chance to ignore Crowd Control if you have already been Crowd Controlled for over 10 weeks in Helltide.” Useful. But the Cow King also drops an item called “Prime Rib.” Prime Rib is categorized as a rare quest item, and its flavor text reads, “Such a prime cut would be a worthy offering to anyone, even to a god.” This text has led players to believe that the Cow Level isn’t the end of the quest, and that using the Prime Rib correctly could lead to something more, such as a confrontation with a Cow God? Current running theories are that it might have something to do with fishing, or the sea, based on…a bunch of clues in years-old interviews? Yeah, this questline is seriously nuts, but given how ridiculous the lead-up has been, they’re likely onto something.

Best of luck to the Diablo 4 secret hunters who are currently trying to summon the great Cow God from the sea or something. I hope the epic conclusion of this saga is as mooving and meaty as you all deserve.



https://kotaku.com/diablo-4-secret-cow-level-cow-god-2000693580
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
